passage.title: How a Letter Travels
passage.subject: ELA
passage.text:
Have you ever wondered what happens to a letter after you drop it in a mailbox? A single letter takes a long journey before it reaches the right home, and many workers and machines help it along the way.

First, you write your letter, fold it into an envelope, and add a stamp. The stamp is like a ticket that pays for the trip. Then you drop the letter into a blue mailbox on the street, or you leave it in your own mailbox and raise the flag. A raised flag can show that outgoing mail is waiting. Writing the address neatly helps every machine and worker send the letter to the right place.

Soon a mail carrier comes by and collects the waiting letters. The carrier brings them to a building called a sorting center. A sorting center is a large place where mail from many neighborhoods comes together at once. Inside, tall machines read the address and the ZIP code printed on each envelope. A ZIP code is a set of numbers that stands for a certain area of the country. The machines can sort thousands of letters in an hour — far faster than people could ever sort them by hand. A barcode may be printed near the bottom of the envelope. Sorting machines read the barcode to help send the letter toward the correct area. Workers watch over the machines and pull out any letter that is bent, torn, or hard to read, so every piece keeps moving toward its home.

After they are sorted, the letters travel toward the city or town written on the address. Letters going a short distance ride in trucks. Letters headed far away may even fly across the country on an airplane. During this part of the trip, mail may move by road or air across the postal network before continuing toward its final destination. The letters ride in sturdy trays and bins that keep them standing upright and in the right order. At every step, each letter is grouped with other letters going to the same place. Grouping letters by destination helps keep the mail organized.

When the letters reach the local post office, carriers sort them one more time — now by street and by house number. Finally, a carrier loads the mail and follows a route through the neighborhood, slipping each letter into the correct box.

So the next time a letter lands in your mailbox, remember the trip it took. It was collected, read by machines, carried across town, and delivered to your door, all because of a small stamp and a careful plan.

factCheckNotesJson.claim: A postage stamp is affixed to mail to prepay the cost of delivery.
A ZIP Code's digits identify a geographic area of the country, narrowing from region to local Post Office.
factCheckNotesJson.sourceTitle: Introduction of the ZIP Code – U.S. Postal Facts

factCheckNotesJson.claim: At modern processing centers mail is faced, cancelled, and sorted by automated machinery rather than by hand.
Mail moves through the air and along roads in trucks (and over the sea) across the postal transportation network.
ZIP+4 and added digits let mail be sorted by destination to a specific street, then to a residence or business (finer destination-based sorting).
factCheckNotesJson.sourceTitle: Introduction of the ZIP Code – U.S. Postal Facts

passage.title: A Mail Carrier's Day
passage.subject: ELA
passage.text:
A mail carrier helps every letter finish its journey. But the job takes much more than walking down the street and dropping off mail. A good carrier uses special tools and follows a careful plan, rain or shine.

The day begins early at the post office. Before heading out, the carrier sorts the mail into the exact order of the route. A route is the planned path a carrier follows through a neighborhood. By putting the mail in order first, the carrier never has to stop and dig around for the next house's letters. Putting the mail in route order takes time, but it saves time later on the street. Over time, a carrier learns the neighborhood well and remembers which homes are easy to miss.

On walking routes, a carrier may use a strong shoulder bag called a satchel. It holds letters, magazines, and small packages. On some curbside routes, carriers use postal vehicles designed to make roadside mailboxes easier to reach.

Carriers also carry a small scanner. When a package must be tracked or needs a signature, the carrier scans it. The scan records information about the package and updates its tracking record, so the family waiting for it can follow its trip. Some packages are too big for the satchel and ride in the truck until the carrier reaches the right address, where the carrier checks the label twice to be sure it goes to the correct home.

Mail carriers work in every kind of weather. They wear sturdy shoes for the long walks and bring water on hot days. They watch carefully for dogs, icy steps, and busy streets. In winter they knock the snow from their boots; in summer they wear hats to block the sun. A friendly wave from a neighbor, or a thank-you note tucked in a mailbox, can make a long day feel lighter. The work is not easy, yet carriers take real pride in bringing the mail to the right home, day after day.

Because of a carrier's patient work, a letter that began as one small envelope finally reaches the very person who has been waiting to read it.

factCheckNotesJson.claim: A city carrier's route includes office duties such as casing mail before street delivery (office vs. street operations; ~80% of the day on the street).
factCheckNotesJson.sourceTitle: City Delivery Operations – Nationwide Route Management

factCheckNotesJson.claim: Carriers carry mail in a letter-carrier satchel.
City carriers deliver and collect mail on foot or by vehicle.
Carriers use a handheld Mobile Delivery Device (MDD) to scan packages and transmit real-time tracking data.
City carriers deliver under varying road and weather conditions ("in all kinds of weather").

choices.rationale: Correct. Text 1 and Text 2 both show organization helping mail reach the correct home.

choices.text: The mail system works without a careful plan or organized order.
choices.rationale: This reverses both texts, which emphasize planning and organization.

choices.text: Both texts are mainly about stamps.
choices.rationale: Stamps are in Text 1, but they are not the shared idea of both texts.

partB.instruction: Choose two sentences — one from each passage — that best support the answer to Part A.

choices.text: When the letters reach the local post office, carriers sort them one more time — now by street and by house number.
The stamp is like a ticket that pays for the trip.
A good carrier uses special tools and follows a careful plan, rain or shine.
On walking routes, a carrier may use a strong shoulder bag called a satchel.

items.subject: ELA

responseSpecJson.prompt: In Text 1, what does the comparison "The stamp is like a ticket that pays for the trip" mean?
choices: A stamp is an actual paper ticket that a person tears.
A stamp shows the address where the letter should go.
A stamp makes the letter travel faster through machines.
A stamp is the payment that lets the letter be carried.

A stamp is the payment that lets the letter be carried.

answerChoicesJson.text: A stamp is a real paper ticket that a person tears off and keeps.
answerChoicesJson.rationale: This reads the comparison literally instead of as a way to explain the stamp's job.

items.subject: ELA

responseSpecJson.prompt: Which statement best describes the author's point of view about a mail carrier's job in Text 2?
choices: The author thinks the job is easy because carriers follow the same route every day.
The author thinks the job is demanding but useful and important.
The author thinks the job is boring and unimportant.
The author mostly admires how sorting machines read ZIP codes.

structuredChoicesJson.text: The author thinks the job is easy because carriers follow the same route every day.
The author thinks the job is demanding but useful and important.
The author thinks the job is boring and unimportant.
The author mostly admires how sorting machines read ZIP codes.

evidenceBinding.quotedSpan: The work is not easy, yet carriers take real pride in bringing the mail to the right home, day after day.

items.studentFacingPrompt: Which statement best describes the author's point of view about a mail carrier's job in Text 2?
items.stem: Which statement best describes the author's point of view about a mail carrier's job in Text 2?

answerChoicesJson.text: The author thinks the job is easy because carriers follow the same route every day.
answerChoicesJson.rationale: This reverses the author's view that the work is not easy.
answerChoicesJson.evidence: The work is not easy, yet carriers take real pride in bringing the mail to the right home, day after day.

evidenceLinks.quotedSpan: The work is not easy, yet carriers take real pride in bringing the mail to the right home, day after day.

answerChoicesJson.text: The author thinks the job is demanding but useful and important.
answerChoicesJson.rationale: Correct. Text 2 describes hard conditions and pride in delivering mail.

partA.prompt: According to Text 2, why does the carrier put the mail in route order before leaving?

choices.text: It saves time because the carrier does not have to dig for each house's letters later.
It makes the satchel lighter before the carrier starts walking.
It helps the carrier slowly memorize many house addresses across the neighborhood over time.
It causes the carrier to stop more often on the street.

partB.instruction: Choose two sentences from "A Mail Carrier's Day" that best support the answer.

choices.text: A route is the planned path a carrier follows through a neighborhood.
By putting the mail in order first, the carrier never has to stop and dig around for the next house's letters.
Over time, a carrier learns the neighborhood well and remembers which homes are easy to miss.
Putting the mail in route order takes time, but it saves time later on the street.

partA.prompt: According to Text 2, why does the carrier put the mail in route order before leaving?

choices.text: It saves time because the carrier does not have to dig for each house's letters later.
choices.rationale: Correct. The paragraph explains that route order prevents stopping and digging and saves time.

choices.text: It makes the satchel lighter before the carrier starts walking.
choices.rationale: This uses a tool detail but does not explain the route-order cause and effect.

choices.text: It helps the carrier slowly memorize many house addresses across the neighborhood over time.
choices.rationale: The text says carriers learn the neighborhood, but it does not say route order is for memorizing every address.

choices.text: It causes the carrier to stop more often on the street.
choices.rationale: This reverses the effect because route order saves time and prevents extra digging.

choices.text: A route is the planned path a carrier follows through a neighborhood.
By putting the mail in order first, the carrier never has to stop and dig around for the next house's letters.
Over time, a carrier learns the neighborhood well and remembers which homes are easy to miss.
Putting the mail in route order takes time, but it saves time later on the street.
